"You weren't there."

"I was. I just— I didn't want to be seen."

"Why?"

He looked at his hands. "Because I wasn't sure what I'd do, if I saw her."

"Do what?"

"Anything. Walk over. Say something. Pretend I'd come for the coffee."

"It's a coffee shop," she said. "People come for the coffee."

"You know what I mean."

"I don't, actually. That's the whole problem—you keep doing this thing where you assume I follow. I don't."

He was quiet for a long time. Outside, a delivery truck reversed, beeped twice, gave up. Somewhere upstairs a child was crying and a different adult was trying to settle it with low, patient sentences that were almost lost under the wheeze of the fan.

"Okay," he said. "Okay. I'll start over. From the beginning."

"From which beginning?"

"The first one. When I told you I wasn't going to come back."

"That wasn't a beginning. That was the middle."

"Fine. The middle, then. Whatever you want to call it."
